headline: California's Hidden Solar Farms: Canals That Generate Power and Save Water
meta_description: Solar panels above California irrigation canals cut water loss by 70% while generating renewable energy. Project Nexus shows how dual-purpose infrastructure co…
summary_short: Shade from solar canopies above California's irrigation canals reduces water evaporation by 70% and cuts algae growth by 85%, offering an unexpected bonus to renewable e…
body:
California's roughly 4,000 miles of open irrigation canals were designed for one job: delivering water to farms and homes. Project Nexus discovered they can do two.

When researchers installed solar canopies above two canals south of Modesto as part of a state-funded pilot, they expected to measure power generation. What they found was equally remarkable: shade from the panels reduced water evaporation by up to 70 percent and cut aquatic weed and algae growth by up to 85 percent over a full irrigation season.

The discovery reframes the economics entirely. While solar canals cost more to build than ground-mounted desert solar, the additional benefits—conserved water, reduced maintenance burden from weeds, and saved land—may justify the investment. For canal operators spending considerable resources managing algae and weeds, an 85 percent reduction is transformative.

A UC Merced study modeled covering all of California's major canals with solar panels: 13 gigawatts of generating capacity and 63 billion gallons of water saved annually—enough to serve 2 million people. Even if only a fraction gets built, the gains are substantial.

The Gila River Indian Community recently completed a solar canal project south of Phoenix, producing 25 percent more power than projected, possibly because water cools the panels from below. Success stories from Gujarat to Arizona suggest California's experiment could reshape how the American West manages both water and energy.
